lines: And I in going, madam, weep o'er my
father's death anew; but I must attend his Majesty's
command, to whom I am now in ward, evermore
in subjection.
fullText: And I in going, madam, weep o'er my father's death anew; but I must attend his Majesty's command, to whom I am now in ward, evermore in subjection.

lines: What is it, my good lord, the King languishes
of?
fullText: What is it, my good lord, the King languishes of?

lines: I heard not of it before.
fullText: I heard not of it before.

lines: Madam, I desire your holy wishes.
fullText: Madam, I desire your holy wishes.

lines: The best wishes that can be forged in your
thoughts be servants to you.
mistress, and make much of her.
fullText: The best wishes that can be forged in your thoughts be servants to you. mistress, and make much of her.

lines: My thanks and duty are your Majesty's.
fullText: My thanks and duty are your Majesty's.

lines: His good remembrance, sir,
Lies richer in your thoughts than on his tomb.
So in approof lives not his epitaph
As in your royal speech.
fullText: His good remembrance, sir, Lies richer in your thoughts than on his tomb. So in approof lives not his epitaph As in your royal speech.

lines: Some six months since, my lord.
fullText: Some six months since, my lord.

lines: Thank your Majesty.
fullText: Thank your Majesty.

lines: I am commanded here and kept a coil
With "Too young," and "The next year," and "'Tis
too early."
fullText: I am commanded here and kept a coil With "Too young," and "The next year," and "'Tis too early."

lines: I shall stay here the forehorse to a smock,
Creaking my shoes on the plain masonry
Till honor be bought up, and no sword worn
But one to dance with. By heaven, I'll steal away!
fullText: I shall stay here the forehorse to a smock, Creaking my shoes on the plain masonry Till honor be bought up, and no sword worn But one to dance with. By heaven, I'll steal away!

lines: I grow to you, and our parting is a tortured
body.
fullText: I grow to you, and our parting is a tortured body.

lines: Stay the King.
fullText: Stay the King.

lines: And I will do so.
fullText: And I will do so.

lines: And so 'tis.
fullText: And so 'tis.

lines: My wife, my liege? I shall beseech your Highness
In such a business give me leave to use
The help of mine own eyes.
fullText: My wife, my liege? I shall beseech your Highness In such a business give me leave to use The help of mine own eyes.

lines: Yes, my good lord,
But never hope to know why I should marry her.
fullText: Yes, my good lord, But never hope to know why I should marry her.

lines: But follows it, my lord, to bring me down
Must answer for your raising? I know her well;
She had her breeding at my father's charge.
A poor physician's daughter my wife? Disdain
Rather corrupt me ever!
fullText: But follows it, my lord, to bring me down Must answer for your raising? I know her well; She had her breeding at my father's charge. A poor physician's daughter my wife? Disdain Rather corrupt me ever!

lines: I cannot love her, nor will strive to do 't.
fullText: I cannot love her, nor will strive to do 't.

lines: Pardon, my gracious lord, for I submit
My fancy to your eyes. When I consider
What great creation and what dole of honor
Flies where you bid it, I find that she which late
Was in my nobler thoughts most base is now
The praised of the King, who, so ennobled,
Is as 'twere born so.
fullText: Pardon, my gracious lord, for I submit My fancy to your eyes. When I consider What great creation and what dole of honor Flies where you bid it, I find that she which late Was in my nobler thoughts most base is now The praised of the King, who, so ennobled, Is as 'twere born so.

lines: I take her hand.
fullText: I take her hand.

lines: Undone, and forfeited to cares forever!
fullText: Undone, and forfeited to cares forever!

lines: Although before the solemn priest I have sworn,
I will not bed her.
fullText: Although before the solemn priest I have sworn, I will not bed her.

lines: O my Parolles, they have married me!
I'll to the Tuscan wars and never bed her.
fullText: O my Parolles, they have married me! I'll to the Tuscan wars and never bed her.

lines: There's letters from my mother. What th'
import is I know not yet.
fullText: There's letters from my mother. What th' import is I know not yet.

lines: It shall be so. I'll send her to my house,
Acquaint my mother with my hate to her
And wherefore I am fled, write to the King
That which I durst not speak. His present gift
Shall furnish me to those Italian fields
Where noble fellows strike. Wars is no strife
To the dark house and the detested wife.
fullText: It shall be so. I'll send her to my house, Acquaint my mother with my hate to her And wherefore I am fled, write to the King That which I durst not speak. His present gift Shall furnish me to those Italian fields Where noble fellows strike. Wars is no strife To the dark house and the detested wife.

lines: Go with me to my chamber, and advise me.
I'll send her straight away. Tomorrow
I'll to the wars, she to her single sorrow.
fullText: Go with me to my chamber, and advise me. I'll send her straight away. Tomorrow I'll to the wars, she to her single sorrow.

lines: Yes, my lord, and of very valiant approof.
fullText: Yes, my lord, and of very valiant approof.

lines: And by other warranted testimony.
fullText: And by other warranted testimony.

lines: I do assure you, my lord, he is very great in
knowledge and accordingly valiant.
fullText: I do assure you, my lord, he is very great in knowledge and accordingly valiant.

lines: Is she gone to the King?
fullText: Is she gone to the King?

lines: Will she away tonight?
fullText: Will she away tonight?

lines: I have writ my letters, casketed my treasure,
Given order for our horses, and tonight,
When I should take possession of the bride,
End ere I do begin.
fullText: I have writ my letters, casketed my treasure, Given order for our horses, and tonight, When I should take possession of the bride, End ere I do begin.

lines: Is there any unkindness
between my lord and you, monsieur?
fullText: Is there any unkindness between my lord and you, monsieur?

lines: It may be you have mistaken him, my lord.
fullText: It may be you have mistaken him, my lord.

lines: I think not so.
fullText: I think not so.

lines: Yes, I do know him well, and common speech
Gives him a worthy pass.
fullText: Yes, I do know him well, and common speech Gives him a worthy pass.

lines: I shall obey his will.
You must not marvel, Helen, at my course,
Which holds not color with the time, nor does
The ministration and required office
On my particular. Prepared I was not
For such a business; therefore am I found
So much unsettled. This drives me to entreat you
That presently you take your way for home,
And rather muse than ask why I entreat you;
For my respects are better than they seem,
And my appointments have in them a need
Greater than shows itself at the first view
To you that know them not.
This to my mother.
'Twill be two days ere I shall see you, so
I leave you to your wisdom.
fullText: I shall obey his will. You must not marvel, Helen, at my course, Which holds not color with the time, nor does The ministration and required office On my particular. Prepared I was not For such a business; therefore am I found So much unsettled. This drives me to entreat you That presently you take your way for home, And rather muse than ask why I entreat you; For my respects are better than they seem, And my appointments have in them a need Greater than shows itself at the first view To you that know them not. This to my mother. 'Twill be two days ere I shall see you, so I leave you to your wisdom.

lines: Come, come, no more of that.
fullText: Come, come, no more of that.

lines: Let that go.
My haste is very great. Farewell. Hie home.
fullText: Let that go. My haste is very great. Farewell. Hie home.

lines: Well, what would you say?
fullText: Well, what would you say?

lines: What would you have?
fullText: What would you have?

lines: I pray you stay not, but in haste to horse.
fullText: I pray you stay not, but in haste to horse.

lines: Go thou toward home, where I will never come
Whilst I can shake my sword or hear the drum.--
Away, and for our flight.
fullText: Go thou toward home, where I will never come Whilst I can shake my sword or hear the drum.-- Away, and for our flight.

lines: Sir, it is
A charge too heavy for my strength, but yet
We'll strive to bear it for your worthy sake
To th' extreme edge of hazard.
fullText: Sir, it is A charge too heavy for my strength, but yet We'll strive to bear it for your worthy sake To th' extreme edge of hazard.

lines: This very day,
Great Mars, I put myself into thy file.
Make me but like my thoughts, and I shall prove
A lover of thy drum, hater of love.
fullText: This very day, Great Mars, I put myself into thy file. Make me but like my thoughts, and I shall prove A lover of thy drum, hater of love.

lines: Do you think I am so far deceived in him?
fullText: Do you think I am so far deceived in him?

lines: I would I knew in what particular action to
try him.
fullText: I would I knew in what particular action to try him.

lines: How now, monsieur? This
drum sticks sorely in your disposition.
fullText: How now, monsieur? This drum sticks sorely in your disposition.

lines: Well, we cannot greatly condemn our success.
Some dishonor we had in the loss of that
drum, but it is not to be recovered.
fullText: Well, we cannot greatly condemn our success. Some dishonor we had in the loss of that drum, but it is not to be recovered.

lines: It might, but it is not now.
fullText: It might, but it is not now.

lines: Why, if you have a stomach, to 't, monsieur!
If you think your mystery in stratagem can bring
this instrument of honor again into his native
quarter, be magnanimous in the enterprise and go
on. I will grace the attempt for a worthy exploit. If
you speed well in it, the Duke shall both speak of it
and extend to you what further becomes his greatness,
even to the utmost syllable of your
worthiness.
fullText: Why, if you have a stomach, to 't, monsieur! If you think your mystery in stratagem can bring this instrument of honor again into his native quarter, be magnanimous in the enterprise and go on. I will grace the attempt for a worthy exploit. If you speed well in it, the Duke shall both speak of it and extend to you what further becomes his greatness, even to the utmost syllable of your worthiness.

lines: But you must not now slumber in it.
fullText: But you must not now slumber in it.

lines: May I be bold to acquaint his Grace you are
gone about it?
fullText: May I be bold to acquaint his Grace you are gone about it?

lines: I know thou 'rt valiant, and to the possibility
of thy soldiership will subscribe for thee. Farewell.
fullText: I know thou 'rt valiant, and to the possibility of thy soldiership will subscribe for thee. Farewell.

lines: Why, do you think he will make no deed at
all of this that so seriously he does address himself
unto?
fullText: Why, do you think he will make no deed at all of this that so seriously he does address himself unto?

lines: Your brother he shall go along with me.
fullText: Your brother he shall go along with me.

lines: Now will I lead you to the house and show you
The lass I spoke of.
fullText: Now will I lead you to the house and show you The lass I spoke of.

lines: That's all the fault. I spoke with her but once
And found her wondrous cold. But I sent to her,
By this same coxcomb that we have i' th' wind,
Tokens and letters, which she did re-send.
And this is all I have done. She's a fair creature.
Will you go see her?
fullText: That's all the fault. I spoke with her but once And found her wondrous cold. But I sent to her, By this same coxcomb that we have i' th' wind, Tokens and letters, which she did re-send. And this is all I have done. She's a fair creature. Will you go see her?

lines: They told me that your name was Fontibell.
fullText: They told me that your name was Fontibell.

lines: Titled goddess,
And worth it, with addition. But, fair soul,
In your fine frame hath love no quality?
If the quick fire of youth light not your mind,
You are no maiden but a monument.
When you are dead, you should be such a one
As you are now, for you are cold and stern,
And now you should be as your mother was
When your sweet self was got.
fullText: Titled goddess, And worth it, with addition. But, fair soul, In your fine frame hath love no quality? If the quick fire of youth light not your mind, You are no maiden but a monument. When you are dead, you should be such a one As you are now, for you are cold and stern, And now you should be as your mother was When your sweet self was got.

lines: So should you be.
fullText: So should you be.

lines: No more o' that.
I prithee do not strive against my vows.
I was compelled to her, but I love thee
By love's own sweet constraint, and will forever
Do thee all rights of service.
fullText: No more o' that. I prithee do not strive against my vows. I was compelled to her, but I love thee By love's own sweet constraint, and will forever Do thee all rights of service.

lines: How have I sworn!
fullText: How have I sworn!

lines: Change it, change it.
Be not so holy-cruel. Love is holy,
And my integrity ne'er knew the crafts
That you do charge men with. Stand no more off,
But give thyself unto my sick desires,
Who then recovers. Say thou art mine, and ever
My love as it begins shall so persever.
fullText: Change it, change it. Be not so holy-cruel. Love is holy, And my integrity ne'er knew the crafts That you do charge men with. Stand no more off, But give thyself unto my sick desires, Who then recovers. Say thou art mine, and ever My love as it begins shall so persever.

lines: I'll lend it thee, my dear, but have no power
To give it from me.
fullText: I'll lend it thee, my dear, but have no power To give it from me.

lines: It is an honor 'longing to our house,
Bequeathed down from many ancestors,
Which were the greatest obloquy i' th' world
In me to lose.
fullText: It is an honor 'longing to our house, Bequeathed down from many ancestors, Which were the greatest obloquy i' th' world In me to lose.

lines: Here, take my ring.
My house, mine honor, yea, my life be thine,
And I'll be bid by thee.
fullText: Here, take my ring. My house, mine honor, yea, my life be thine, And I'll be bid by thee.

lines: A heaven on Earth I have won by wooing thee.
fullText: A heaven on Earth I have won by wooing thee.

lines: I have tonight dispatched sixteen businesses,
a month's length apiece. By an abstract of
success: I have congeed with the Duke, done my
adieu with his nearest, buried a wife, mourned for
her, writ to my lady mother I am returning, entertained
my convoy, and between these main parcels
of dispatch effected many nicer needs. The last
was the greatest, but that I have not ended yet.
fullText: I have tonight dispatched sixteen businesses, a month's length apiece. By an abstract of success: I have congeed with the Duke, done my adieu with his nearest, buried a wife, mourned for her, writ to my lady mother I am returning, entertained my convoy, and between these main parcels of dispatch effected many nicer needs. The last was the greatest, but that I have not ended yet.

lines: I mean the business is not ended as fearing
to hear of it hereafter. But shall we have this dialogue
between the Fool and the Soldier? Come,
bring forth this counterfeit module; has deceived
me like a double-meaning prophesier.
fullText: I mean the business is not ended as fearing to hear of it hereafter. But shall we have this dialogue between the Fool and the Soldier? Come, bring forth this counterfeit module; has deceived me like a double-meaning prophesier.

lines: No matter. His heels have deserved it in
usurping his spurs so long. How does he carry
himself?
fullText: No matter. His heels have deserved it in usurping his spurs so long. How does he carry himself?

lines: Nothing of me, has he?
fullText: Nothing of me, has he?

lines: A plague upon him! Muffled! He can say
nothing of me.
fullText: A plague upon him! Muffled! He can say nothing of me.

lines: All's one to him. What a past-saving
slave is this!
fullText: All's one to him. What a past-saving slave is this!

lines: But I con him no thanks for 't, in the
nature he delivers it.
fullText: But I con him no thanks for 't, in the nature he delivers it.

lines: What shall be done to him?
fullText: What shall be done to him?

lines: Nay, by your leave, hold
your hands, though I know his brains are forfeit to
the next tile that falls.
fullText: Nay, by your leave, hold your hands, though I know his brains are forfeit to the next tile that falls.

lines: Our interpreter does it well.
fullText: Our interpreter does it well.

lines: Damnable both-sides rogue!
fullText: Damnable both-sides rogue!

lines: He shall be whipped through the
army with this rhyme in 's forehead.
fullText: He shall be whipped through the army with this rhyme in 's forehead.

lines: I could endure anything before but a
cat, and now he's a cat to me.
fullText: I could endure anything before but a cat, and now he's a cat to me.

lines: For this description of thine honesty?
A pox upon him! For me, he's more and more
a cat.
fullText: For this description of thine honesty? A pox upon him! For me, he's more and more a cat.

lines: A pox on him! He's a cat still.
fullText: A pox on him! He's a cat still.

lines: Good morrow, noble captain.
fullText: Good morrow, noble captain.

lines: My high-repented blames,
Dear sovereign, pardon to me.
fullText: My high-repented blames, Dear sovereign, pardon to me.

lines: Admiringly, my liege. At first
I stuck my choice upon her, ere my heart
Durst make too bold a herald of my tongue;
Where the impression of mine eye infixing,
Contempt his scornful perspective did lend me,
Which warped the line of every other favor,
Scorned a fair color or expressed it stol'n,
Extended or contracted all proportions
To a most hideous object. Thence it came
That she whom all men praised and whom myself,
Since I have lost, have loved, was in mine eye
The dust that did offend it.
fullText: Admiringly, my liege. At first I stuck my choice upon her, ere my heart Durst make too bold a herald of my tongue; Where the impression of mine eye infixing, Contempt his scornful perspective did lend me, Which warped the line of every other favor, Scorned a fair color or expressed it stol'n, Extended or contracted all proportions To a most hideous object. Thence it came That she whom all men praised and whom myself, Since I have lost, have loved, was in mine eye The dust that did offend it.

lines: Hers it was not.
fullText: Hers it was not.

lines: My gracious
sovereign,
Howe'er it pleases you to take it so,
The ring was never hers.
fullText: My gracious sovereign, Howe'er it pleases you to take it so, The ring was never hers.

lines: You are deceived, my lord. She never saw it.
In Florence was it from a casement thrown me,
Wrapped in a paper which contained the name
Of her that threw it. Noble she was, and thought
I stood ungaged, but when I had subscribed
To mine own fortune and informed her fully
I could not answer in that course of honor
As she had made the overture, she ceased
In heavy satisfaction and would never
Receive the ring again.
fullText: You are deceived, my lord. She never saw it. In Florence was it from a casement thrown me, Wrapped in a paper which contained the name Of her that threw it. Noble she was, and thought I stood ungaged, but when I had subscribed To mine own fortune and informed her fully I could not answer in that course of honor As she had made the overture, she ceased In heavy satisfaction and would never Receive the ring again.

lines: She never saw it.
fullText: She never saw it.

lines: If you shall prove
This ring was ever hers, you shall as easy
Prove that I husbanded her bed in Florence,
Where yet she never was.
fullText: If you shall prove This ring was ever hers, you shall as easy Prove that I husbanded her bed in Florence, Where yet she never was.

lines: My lord, I neither can nor will deny
But that I know them. Do they charge me further?
fullText: My lord, I neither can nor will deny But that I know them. Do they charge me further?

lines: She's none of mine, my lord.
fullText: She's none of mine, my lord.

lines: My lord, this is a fond and desp'rate creature
Whom sometime I have laughed with. Let your
Highness
Lay a more noble thought upon mine honor
Than for to think that I would sink it here.
fullText: My lord, this is a fond and desp'rate creature Whom sometime I have laughed with. Let your Highness Lay a more noble thought upon mine honor Than for to think that I would sink it here.

lines: She's impudent, my lord,
And was a common gamester to the camp.
fullText: She's impudent, my lord, And was a common gamester to the camp.

lines: What of him?
He's quoted for a most perfidious slave,
With all the spots o' th' world taxed and debauched,
Whose nature sickens but to speak a truth.
Am I or that or this for what he'll utter,
That will speak anything?
fullText: What of him? He's quoted for a most perfidious slave, With all the spots o' th' world taxed and debauched, Whose nature sickens but to speak a truth. Am I or that or this for what he'll utter, That will speak anything?

lines: I think she has. Certain it is I liked her
And boarded her i' th' wanton way of youth.
She knew her distance and did angle for me,
Madding my eagerness with her restraint,
As all impediments in fancy's course
Are motives of more fancy; and in fine
Her infinite cunning with her modern grace
Subdued me to her rate. She got the ring,
And I had that which any inferior might
At market price have bought.
fullText: I think she has. Certain it is I liked her And boarded her i' th' wanton way of youth. She knew her distance and did angle for me, Madding my eagerness with her restraint, As all impediments in fancy's course Are motives of more fancy; and in fine Her infinite cunning with her modern grace Subdued me to her rate. She got the ring, And I had that which any inferior might At market price have bought.

lines: I have it not.
fullText: I have it not.

lines: My lord, I do confess the ring was hers.
fullText: My lord, I do confess the ring was hers.

lines: Both, both. O, pardon!
fullText: Both, both. O, pardon!

lines: If she, my liege, can make me know this clearly,
I'll love her dearly, ever, ever dearly.
fullText: If she, my liege, can make me know this clearly, I'll love her dearly, ever, ever dearly.
